required_steps.monitor_progress_toward_iep_goals_progre:
Quarterly review of Malik's IEP goals completed with the special education teacher (Ms. Hernandez), school psychologist (Dr. Kim), and transition coordinator (Mr. Lewis) on 2025-02-10. We reviewed progress data from September 2024 through early February 2025. Malik has met or is on track to meet 9 out of 11 current IEP goals.

Academics: He is making steady gains in functional reading (currently reading simple functional texts such as menus, bus schedules, and short workplace signs at approximately a late 2nd-grade level) and functional math (money skills up to $20 with visual supports, telling time to the quarter hour, and basic one-step word problems when supported with pictures or manipulatives). Data logs show 80–85% accuracy for money identification and making change up to $5 with prompting.

Communication & Social Skills: Malik continues to use his AAC app (Proloquo2Go) more independently. Staff reports that he now initiates greetings with familiar adults and can ask for help in 3–4 word phrases (e.g., "I need help now" or "break please") with minimal prompting. He has met his goal of participating in a structured group activity twice per week with no more than one verbal prompt.

Daily Living & Vocational Skills: In the life skills classroom, Malik is able to complete a basic three‑step task (e.g., wipe table, fold towels, place in bin) with visual schedule support and only one verbal prompt. During community-based instruction, he successfully used his ID card and counted out bus fare with support. At his in-school vocational training site (cafeteria dish room), staff report consistent attendance and improved stamina—able to work 20–25 minutes at a time before requiring a short break.

Behavior & Self-Regulation: Behavioral data show a significant reduction in elopement and refusal behaviors since the introduction of a visual schedule and first/then charts. Average classroom disruption incidents decreased from 3–4 per week last spring to about 1 per week this school year. Malik uses a "calm corner" strategy with headphones and a weighted lap pad when feeling overwhelmed.

Overall, the team agreed Malik is on track to meet his IEP transition and functional skill goals that align with obtaining the Skills and Achievement Commencement Credential in June 2025. Next review planned for late April 2025 to confirm final alignment with exit outcomes.
required_steps.provide_weekly_persistence_coaching_prog:
Weekly persistence coaching sessions have been occurring every Friday since 2024-09-13, typically for 20–30 minutes, either in the resource room or during Malik's study skills period. Sessions are led by his transition coach, Jordan Ellis, in coordination with the school social worker.

Coaching focuses on: (1) attendance and morning routine planning, (2) building self-advocacy (practicing asking teachers for help, asking for breaks appropriately, and using his AAC device to communicate needs), and (3) reinforcing effort and follow-through on his vocational tasks and classroom assignments.

On 2025-02-07, the most recent documented session, Jordan and Malik reviewed his January attendance (3 days absent, 2 late arrivals). They celebrated that this is a reduction from 6 absences in October. Malik identified that going to bed earlier and laying out his clothes the night before helped him get to school on time. They used a simple visual checklist to plan his morning routine (wake up, bathroom, clothes, breakfast, bus card check, backpack check) and practiced saying "I need help" on his AAC app as a persistence strategy when he feels stuck on a task.

Jordan also used role-play to help Malik practice what to do when he feels frustrated at his cafeteria work site: using his "break" card, going to the calm corner for 5 minutes, and then returning to the task. Malik responded well to praise and small tangible rewards (stickers of his favorite basketball team) for meeting weekly goals. Overall, weekly coaching is helping Malik connect his daily actions (showing up, trying again, asking for help) to the larger goal of finishing the school year and receiving his Skills and Achievement Commencement Credential.

potential_steps.improve_attendance_mornings_progress_not:
Morning attendance supports started in mid-September 2024 after Malik had 4 absences and 3 tardies in the first three weeks of school. The coach and foster parent collaborated to create a consistent morning routine, including a 6:45 a.m. phone alarm with a custom ringtone Malik chose himself, preparing his backpack and clothes the night before, and using a visual schedule posted on his bedroom wall.

Beginning 2024-10-01, the coach initiated daily 7:00 a.m. text reminders to Malik's foster mother, Ms. Foster, with brief encouraging messages for Malik (e.g., "Malik, game day at school today—don’t miss it!" or "You’re close to your credential—see you in class!"). On days when Malik seemed reluctant to get up, Ms. Foster texted the coach, who then followed up with a short motivational voice message for Malik, reminding him of his goal to complete school and start a supported employment program.

By December 2024, attendance improved from approximately 80% to around 92%. Due to this improvement, daily morning texts were reduced to three times per week (Monday, Wednesday, Friday) starting in January 2025, with the option to resume daily messages if attendance slips. Malik reports that seeing the calendar countdown to "Graduation in June" on his bedroom wall helps him feel like school is important and worth the effort to get up on time.
potential_steps.improve_attendance_peer_group_progress_n:
On 2024-10-15, Malik was connected with a small, school-based social skills and peer support group facilitated by the school social worker. The group meets every Tuesday during lunch and includes 5–7 students with IEPs who are also in transition planning. One of the older students, Andre (age 19), has already completed his Skills and Achievement Commencement Credential and returned as a peer mentor. Andre has been particularly impactful in talking with Malik about why showing up to school matters and how his own attendance improved when he had something to look forward to at school.

Malik initially sat quietly and needed prompts to engage. Over several weeks, he began responding to simple questions using his AAC device and then started greeting peers independently. The group incorporates games, role-plays, and discussions about future goals. Malik shared (with support) that he wants to work "with cars" or "in a store" and that he likes feeling older when he wears his school ID and carries his backpack.

Staff have noticed that on Tuesdays, Malik is more eager to come to school because he does not want to miss the group. His foster mother reported that he now asks on Monday nights, "Group tomorrow?" which has been a positive motivator for consistent attendance.
potential_steps.improve_attendance_extracurricular_progr:
Malik was connected to the "Unified Basketball" after-school program, which pairs students with and without disabilities on the same team. He began participating on 2024-11-05 and now attends practice twice a week (Tuesdays and Thursdays) from 3:15 p.m. to 4:15 p.m.

Because Malik is a big basketball fan (favorite team: New York Knicks), this program has become a strong attendance incentive. On practice days, he is more motivated to come to school and stay through the afternoon. Staff have noted that he rarely leaves early on those days, and he often reminds his teachers, "Practice today" via his AAC app.

Participation has also supported his social and communication goals: he practices simple phrases like "pass" and "my turn" and follows 1–2 step directions from the coach. When he successfully completes a drill, teammates give him high-fives, which reinforces his confidence and sense of belonging. The coach occasionally uses basketball-themed rewards (e.g., trading cards, stickers) to connect his school attendance and effort with his progress on the team.
potential_steps.speak_meet_program_staff_progress_notes:
On 2025-01-22, the transition coach met with the life skills teacher (Ms. Hernandez), the vocational coordinator (Mr. Lewis), and the cafeteria site supervisor (Ms. Patel) to discuss Malik's attendance, performance, and readiness for completing the Skills and Achievement Commencement Credential.

Key points from the meeting:
- Attendance: Staff confirmed that since November, Malik's attendance to his morning life skills block has been strong, with only occasional absences related to medical appointments.
- Classroom performance: Ms. Hernandez reported that Malik consistently participates in functional academics and daily living instruction when supported with his visual schedule and clear, concrete directions. She noted that he has become more independent with cleaning tasks and simple meal prep activities.
- Vocational performance: At his in-school work site (cafeteria dish room), Ms. Patel observed that Malik is reliable, willing to help, and responds well to praise. He occasionally needs redirection to stay on task, especially when the environment is noisy, but he has not had any major behavioral incidents at the work site.

The team agreed Malik is on track to complete the required in-school vocational experiences for the credential. They also discussed gradually increasing his responsibility—such as stacking trays and wiping tables—with clear visual supports and checklists. The coach will check in monthly with program staff through the end of the school year.
potential_steps.refer_student_tutor_progress_notes:
Due to ongoing challenges in functional reading (especially reading workplace signs and short instructions), Malik was referred to the school’s specialized reading tutor, Ms. Brown, on 2024-10-28. The referral was made by Ms. Hernandez in consultation with the transition coach and the school psychologist.

Ms. Brown provides 1:1 instruction twice a week for 25 minutes, focusing on sight-word recognition of common community and workplace vocabulary (e.g., "exit," "stop," "employees only," "cashier," "restroom"). She uses a combination of picture-word matching, errorless learning, and repeated practice in real-world contexts. Malik responds best when the words are connected to his interests (e.g., basketball gym, snack bar, bus stop).

The goal of the tutoring support is not to bring Malik to grade-level reading but to increase his independence and safety in community and work environments. Early data show improvement: Malik can now reliably identify 15–20 key signs with picture support and is beginning to generalize some of them without pictures in familiar settings around the school.
potential_steps.follow_up_tutor_performance_progress_not:
On 2025-01-10, the transition coach followed up with Ms. Brown (reading tutor) regarding Malik’s progress. Ms. Brown shared progress monitoring data from November 2024 through early January 2025.

Findings:
- Malik has mastered recognition of 18 out of 25 targeted functional words with picture support and 10 out of 25 without picture support in familiar contexts.
- He shows the strongest performance with signs he encounters regularly ("exit," "bathroom," "office," "stop," "bus").
- He still requires significant prompting for less familiar or abstract terms ("warning," "authorized," "employees only").

Ms. Brown noted that Malik is most engaged when lessons are brief, highly visual, and connected to real-life or role-play scenarios (e.g., pretending to clock in at work, reading a "break" sign, or choosing the correct door in a hallway). She recommended continuing tutoring through May 2025 with a slightly narrowed list of highest-priority words that are most relevant to his anticipated supported employment setting (e.g., cafeteria, retail, or custodial environments).

The coach and Ms. Brown agreed to incorporate some of the same visual word cards into Malik’s life skills class and vocational site, so he can practice in multiple settings.
potential_steps.update_foster_parent_caregiver_progress_:
The transition coach maintains regular contact with Malik's foster mother, Angela Foster, through monthly phone calls and text messages. On 2025-01-30, they had a longer check-in call to update her on Malik's progress toward his Skills and Achievement Commencement Credential and to discuss how she can continue to support him at home.

Topics covered:
- Attendance: The coach shared that Malik’s attendance has improved and emphasized how important it is that he continues this through June to complete all required experiences. Ms. Foster agreed to continue using the morning visual schedule and to limit late-night screen time on school nights.
- Behavior and routines: They discussed Malik’s tendency to become overwhelmed when routines change suddenly. Ms. Foster agreed to notify the school in advance of any major appointments or schedule changes so staff can prepare him with visual supports.
- Future planning: The coach explained that the school is working with ACCES-VR to explore supported employment options for Malik after he finishes school. Ms. Foster expressed that she wants him to "have something real to do during the day" and is open to attending meetings to learn more.

Ms. Foster reported that Malik often talks about his basketball team and the cafeteria job and that he seems proud of himself when he brings home positive feedback slips from school. She agreed to continue reinforcing his effort with small rewards at home (extra time to watch basketball highlights, making his favorite dinner, etc.).
potential_steps.ensure_transition_services_progress_note:
During Malik’s annual IEP review on 2024-12-12, the team confirmed that appropriate transition services are in place and aligned with his anticipated post-school outcomes (supported employment, community participation, and increased independence in daily living skills).

Documented transition services include:
- Community-based instruction (CBI) trips twice per month to local grocery stores, the public library, and a nearby shopping plaza to practice using public transportation, handling money, and interacting with community members.
- In-school vocational training in the cafeteria dish room and occasional support tasks for the main office (e.g., shredding paper with supervision, sorting mail).
- Weekly social skills and self-advocacy group focused on communication, understanding workplace expectations, and managing emotions.
- Collaboration with the district’s transition coordinator to connect Malik and his foster parent with adult service agencies (ACCES-VR and OPWDD) prior to school exit.

The IEP includes measurable post-secondary goals (e.g., "After leaving school, Malik will participate in supported employment in a community setting with job coaching" and "Malik will use public transportation with support to access his workplace and community resources"). Transition activities and courses of study (life skills, functional academics, vocational training) clearly support these goals. The team agreed that these services adequately prepare Malik for receiving the Skills and Achievement Commencement Credential and moving into adult services.
potential_steps.ensure_acces_vr_opwdd_meeting_progress_n:
On 2025-01-16, representatives from ACCES-VR and OPWDD joined a transition-focused IEP meeting for Malik via video conference. Present were: Malik (for part of the meeting), his foster mother (Angela Foster), special education teacher (Ms. Hernandez), school psychologist (Dr. Kim), transition coordinator (Mr. Lewis), transition coach (Jordan Ellis), ACCES-VR counselor (Ms. Renee Thompson), and OPWDD care manager liaison (Mr. Carlos Rivera).

ACCES-VR: Ms. Thompson explained the process for applying for vocational rehabilitation services, including documentation needed (psychoeducational evaluations, IEP, medical records) and the likely timeline. She discussed possible supported employment options that match Malik’s strengths (repetitive tasks, willingness to help, positive response to structure), such as working in a cafeteria, grocery store, or custodial crew with job coaching. She confirmed that, assuming eligibility, ACCES-VR can coordinate with a supported employment provider shortly after Malik exits school.

OPWDD: Mr. Rivera described OPWDD eligibility and the range of services (care coordination, day habilitation, supported employment, community integration activities). He suggested starting the application process now so that services can begin smoothly after school exit. He also emphasized the importance of documenting Malik’s need for ongoing supervision and support in community settings.

Action steps: The team agreed that the school psychologist would update Malik’s cognitive and adaptive behavior assessments by March 2025, and the transition coordinator would assist Angela in gathering necessary paperwork for both ACCES-VR and OPWDD applications. Malik stayed for part of the meeting and, with prompting, indicated that he wants to "work with people" and "help" at a job. Both agency representatives expressed that Malik appears to be a good candidate for supported employment services once he completes his Skills and Achievement Commencement Credential.
potential_steps.other: Coordinated practice using public transportation to and from school and work-based learning sites, including travel training with a community mobility instructor.
potential_steps.other_progress_notes:
Starting in November 2024, Malik began participating in structured travel training sessions with the district’s mobility instructor, Ms. O’Connor. Initially, sessions occurred once per month and focused on basic bus safety (waiting behind the curb, boarding in an orderly way, using handrails). As Malik became more comfortable, they progressed to practicing specific routes that he may need in the future, such as traveling from home to a nearby shopping plaza and from home to the local community center.

On 2025-02-03, Malik successfully completed a supervised practice trip from school to the community center using the city bus. With picture-based step cards, he was able to:
- Locate the correct bus stop in front of the school.
- Show his bus pass to the driver.
- Sit in a designated priority seat and remain seated until the correct stop.
- Pull the cord to request the stop, with a prompt from the instructor.

He still needs moderate support to read route numbers and understand changes in schedule, but he is beginning to recognize landmarks near his home and school. The goal is not independent travel but increased familiarity and reduced anxiety when using public transportation with a support person. These experiences also help him generalize functional reading and safety skills learned in the classroom.

goal_tracking.notes:
Malik Jordan is an 18-year-old student in a 12:1:1 life skills program at Riverside High School in Albany, NY. He has a long-standing IEP with classifications related to intellectual disability and speech/language impairment. He participates in the New York State Alternate Assessment and is not working toward a Regents or local diploma; instead, he is on track to earn the Skills and Achievement Commencement Credential in June 2025.

Malik lives with his foster mother, Angela Foster, in a stable foster home placement about 15 minutes from school by city bus. He has been in this home for a little over two years and appears bonded with Ms. Foster, who is very involved and responsive to school communication. Malik’s primary language is English, and he uses a combination of verbal speech (short phrases) and an AAC app on an iPad to communicate.

Strengths: Malik is friendly, enjoys helping others, and responds strongly to positive praise and concrete rewards. He is especially motivated by anything related to basketball, music, and food. He follows routines well when they are clearly laid out with pictures and step-by-step visual supports. At his cafeteria vocational site, he is described as "reliable" and "hardworking" when given clear tasks such as stacking trays, wiping tables, and carrying lightweight items.

Needs: Malik continues to need significant support with reading, writing, complex problem-solving, and managing unexpected changes. He can become anxious or withdrawn when routines shift without warning or when there is a lot of noise. He benefits from having a calm space, headphones, and a trusted adult to help him regulate. He also needs ongoing support to safely navigate community environments and to communicate effectively with unfamiliar adults.

Overall, work toward this goal formally began at the start of the 2024–2025 school year (2024-09-09) with a focus on confirming that Malik is on track for the Skills and Achievement Commencement Credential, strengthening his attendance and engagement, and ensuring that all necessary transition and adult service connections (ACCES-VR, OPWDD) are in progress. At this time (February 2025), Malik remains on track to complete the credential by June 2025, provided he maintains consistent attendance and continues participating in his vocational and life skills programming. The team will revisit goal completion status at the late-spring IEP/transition check-in and update this section with a completion date once the credential is officially conferred.
